coredata.dc:title: High-yield green synthesis of carbon-nanohair/carbon quantum dots/activated carbon composite from agricultural or textile wastes for enhanced supercapacitor performance

coredata.dc:description: A novel approach is demonstrated to fabricate decorated carbon nanohair/carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/activated carbon (ACs) composites from diverse waste sources via thermal activation, omitting the need for inert gas or nitrogen sources, enhancing sustainability, cost-efficiency, decreasing carbon footprint and gaining carbon credit which aligning with Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). The synthesized composites undergo thermal and chemical treatment with potassium hydroxide, resulting in high yield and increasing both the porosity and surface area with values ranged from 1160 m2/g to 1620 m2/g. Notably, Jeans-carbon-composite (JAC) demonstrates outstanding cell-specific capacitance of 988.8 F/g at 0.5 A/g and 0.9 V, with a capacitance retention increasing up to ∼ 260 % over 1200 cycle. Furthermore, at 1.2 V and 0.5 A/g, cell-specific capacitances for other wastes are: Wood-dust-carbon (WAC) = 983.3 F/g, Olive-leaves-carbon (OAC) = 750 F/g, Bagasse-carbon (BAC) = 476.6 F/g, Peanut-shell-carbon (PAC) = 220 F/g and Garlic-leaves-carbon (GAC) = 163.3 F/g. GAC has a negative capacitance-to-potential window relationship, characterized by a high cell-specific capacitance value of 6296 F/g at a low potential of 0.5 V, which declines to 780 F/g at 0.8 V and drops to 163.3 F/g at 1.2 V, demonstrating high energy and power densities simultaneously. The synthesized composites demonstrate compelling electrochemical performance, making them promising supercapacitor electrode materials, owing to their unique hierarchical porous structure.

head.ce:title: High-yield green synthesis of carbon-nanohair/carbon quantum dots/activated carbon composite from agricultural or textile wastes for enhanced supercapacitor performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel approach is demonstrated to fabricate decorated carbon nanohair/carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/activated carbon (ACs) composites from diverse waste sources via thermal activation, omitting the need for inert gas or nitrogen sources, enhancing sustainability, cost-efficiency, decreasing carbon footprint and gaining carbon credit which aligning with Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). The synthesized composites undergo thermal and chemical treatment with potassium hydroxide, resulting in high yield and increasing both the porosity and surface area with values ranged from 1160 m2/g to 1620 m2/g. Notably, Jeans-carbon-composite (JAC) demonstrates outstanding cell-specific capacitance of 988.8 F/g at 0.5 A/g and 0.9 V, with a capacitance retention increasing up to ∼ 260 % over 1200 cycle. Furthermore, at 1.2 V and 0.5 A/g, cell-specific capacitances for other wastes are: Wood-dust-carbon (WAC) = 983.3 F/g, Olive-leaves-carbon (OAC) = 750 F/g, Bagasse-carbon (BAC) = 476.6 F/g, Peanut-shell-carbon (PAC) = 220 F/g and Garlic-leaves-carbon (GAC) = 163.3 F/g. GAC has a negative capacitance-to-potential window relationship, characterized by a high cell-specific capacitance value of 6296 F/g at a low potential of 0.5 V, which declines to 780 F/g at 0.8 V and drops to 163.3 F/g at 1.2 V, demonstrating high energy and power densities simultaneously. The synthesized composites demonstrate compelling electrochemical performance, making them promising supercapacitor electrode materials, owing to their unique hierarchical porous structure.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The current global energy crisis has created an urgent need for innovative and sustainable solutions to meet the rising energy demands while minimizing environmental consequences [1]. Policymakers and industry leaders must work together to address this complex challenge and ensure a stable and prosperous energy future that will prompt many researchers to focus on the transition from non-renewables to different sources of renewable energy [1]. Energy storage devices are critical components in modern energy systems [2]. They are serving as essential components to balance fluctuations in supply and demand, improve the grid stability, and effectively integrate renewable energy sources [2]. Their significance lies in mitigating the intermittent of renewable energy generation [2], ensuring continuous power supply during periods of low production. Additionally, energy storage systems enable peak shaving by storing and releasing energy, reducing strain on power infrastructure and optimizing overall efficiency [2,3]. However, these devices also have limitations, including high initial costs, limited energy density, and some environmental concerns related to the materials used in certain technologies [4]. As technology advances and research progresses, addressing these limitations will be pivotal in unlocking the full potential of energy storage devices and driving the global transition towards a sustainable and resilient energy future [5–7]. So, the usage of eco-friendly materials in energy storage devices and supercapacitors is gaining significant attention as a sustainable solution for the growing energy demands [8]. These environmentally conscious materials offer several advantages, including reduced reliance on hazardous substances, lower carbon footprint, and improved recyclability [9]. Additionally, eco-friendly energy storage technologies exhibit enhanced performance, contributing to more efficient and long-lasting devices. As the world strives to combat climate change and embrace renewable energy, the adoption of such materials in energy storage systems marks a pivotal step towards a greener and cleaner future [10,11].

Effective waste management is an integral component in achieving sustainable development goals. By implementing sound waste management practices, societies can address numerous environmental, social, and economic challenges [12,13]. Proper waste disposal and recycling reduce the burden on landfills and mitigate environmental pollution [14], safeguarding ecosystems and human health [15]. Additionally, recycling initiatives create employment opportunities and stimulate economic growth [16]. Furthermore, waste-to-energy processes can harness resources from waste streams, contributing to renewable energy production and reducing greenhouse gas emissions [17]. By promoting responsible waste management practices, communities can move closer to achieving sustainable development goals, ensuring a cleaner, healthier [18], and more resilient future for generations to come [19].

Agriculture waste in Egypt is a significant environmental challenge and a potential resource for sustainable solutions. Egypt, being an agrarian economy, generates substantial amounts of agricultural residues [20], including crop leftovers, straw, husks [21], and other organic materials [22]. Utilizing agriculture waste in batteries and supercapacitors offers a promising solution for sustainable energy storage [23,24]. Researchers are exploring the conversion of crop residues and husks into carbon-based materials through processes like pyrolysis [8,25], carbonization, and activation [26]. These materials can be integrated into battery electrodes or used as activated carbon in supercapacitors [23,27], providing renewable and cost-effective alternatives to conventional energy storage [28]. This approach reduces landfill waste and lowers greenhouse gas emissions as it is a negative carbon impact, contributing to a greener and more sustainable future for energy technologies [29].

The activation of biochar to produce activated carbon involves various methods that enhance its adsorption capacity and surface area, making it a valuable material for diverse applications [28]. One commonly used technique is physical activation, where biochar in an inert atmosphere is subjected to high temperatures [24], leading to the creation of pores and the expansion of surface area. Chemical activation, on the other hand, involves treating biochar with activating agents like phosphoric acid or potassium hydroxide [14]. Both the two methods go under inert atmosphere which prevent combustion or oxidation of the biochar [30], which leads to the creation of micropores and mesopores, further enhancing its adsorption properties [14]. Another method is steam activation [31], where steam is introduced to biochar at elevated temperatures, resulting in pore formation and increased surface reactivity [31,32]. Additionally, advances in microwave and plasma activation methods have shown promising results in efficiently producing activated carbon with tailored pore structures [33]. These activation methods modify the internal structure of biochar, tailoring it to specific applications such as water purification [34], air filtration [35], and energy storage [27,28,36]. The process of converting biochar to activated carbon unlocks its full potential, turning a once-waste material into a valuable resource for environmentally friendly and sustainable technologies.

For supercapacitor electrodes, activated carbon is a popular material because of its large surface area, conductivity, and chemical stability. So, researchers have continued to explore the use of natural and agricultural activated carbon for supercapacitor applications. In a previous study, Jinxiao et al., onion-based porous carbon was used to fabricate a high-performance supercapacitor at a current density of 0.1 A g−1, with a capacitance value of 568 F g−1 [37]. In 2019, Ouassim et al., developed supercapacitor with high performance from the raw anthracite with a capacitance value of 198.15 F g−1 in aqueous electrolyte and keep its highest energy and power densities [38]. In the research conducted by Ekaterina et al., [39] an examination was carried out on the application of rice husk activated carbon in conjunction with an electrolyte with redox activity for supercapacitor electrodes. The investigation revealed that, at a current density of 0.5 A g−1, the incorporation of redox-active K3Fe[CN]6 into the standard 1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte resulted in a substantial (3.6-fold) augmentation in the specific capacitance of the activated carbon-based electrode, reaching up to 400 F g−1.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have recently attracted extensive attention from researchers in many fields due to their unique properties [39]. CQDs are small carbon nanoparticles (less than 10 nm in size) [40] that have found widespread applications in biomedicine [41], optoelectronics [42], supercapacitors [43] and catalysis [44]. In recent years, the use of CQDs in supercapacitor applications has been investigated, with promising results. Yirong Zhu et al. reported for the first time a new application of CQD-based hybrid as an excellent electrode material for supercapacitors. The CQDs were fabricated by a facile chemical oxidation method following which they were thermally reduced and further decorated with RuO2 to obtain the composites. The hybrid exhibited a specific capacitance of 460 F g −1 at an ultrahigh current density of 50 A g −1 (41.9 wt% Ru loading), and excellent rate capability (88.6, 84.2, and 77.4 % of capacity retention rate at 10, 20, and 50 A g −1 compared with 1 A g −1, respectively). Surprisingly, the hybrid showed exceptional cycling stability with 96.9 % capacity retention over 5000 cycles at 5 A g −1 [45]. Permatasari et al. reviewed the recent advances and future challenges in the use of CQDs for supercapacitor applications.

Nanohair is one of the most appealing materials for supercapacitors because they contribute to create greater design simplicity, a faster charge-transportation network, and more accessible ion diffusion [46] as mentioned in supportinginformation, but there is a limitation in carbon nanohair publications, and there are no existing publications that discuss the performance of carbon nanohair in supercapacitor applications specially.

The present study introduces a novel approach to waste management by synthesizing carbon nanohair/CQDs decorated activated carbon composite from a variety of agriculture and textile waste sources. Our innovative method eliminates the need for inert or nitrogen atmospheres. This advancement not only reduces costs and energy consumption but also transforms discarded materials into valuable resources. Given the absence of previous publications on carbon nanohair fabrication method or applications, especially regarding its performance in supercapacitors and originating from various sources, we opted to maintain the purity of the carbon composite in our study without incorporating additives that could potentially affect or enhance the supercapacitor performance. This approach allows us to provide a comprehensive description of our novel carbon composite, ensuring a clear understanding of its intrinsic properties and capabilities. Over all, by converting these wastes into electrode materials for energy storage devices like supercapacitors, the research presents a sustainable and eco-friendly solution that aligns with advancements in green technology and SDGs.

Various Egyptian agriculture wastes from hard and soft cellulosic origin are used as a precursor for carbonaceous materials. The waste types, the chemical used in this research paper, and the detailed preparation method in S-1: Experimental Section (supporting information).

The obtained carbonaceous product (labeled as Xn, where X represents the material source, and n is abbreviation of Non-Activated), and the activated materials (XAC, where X represents material source, and AC is abbreviation of activated). The detailed process of fabrication is illustrated in Fig. 1 and the preparation method details in S-1: Experimental Section (supporting information).

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra, fluorescence spectra, morphology characterization using SEM, and TEM analyses, thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA), specific surface area characterization using BET, Zeta potential, and the electrochemical performance using Autolab poteniostat device are performed. All these materials characterization and the details of operation are collected in S-1: Experimental Section (supporting information).

The electrochemical activity of porous carbon materials is investigated at room temperature. In a three-electrode system, an Autolab potentiostat is used, with the prepared composite electrode serving as the active electrode, as well as an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode and a Pt foil electrode as the counter electrode. To prepare the electrode, a mixture of the prepared carbon, carbon black, and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVDF) are mixed in an 80:10:10 wt ratios, respectively. As a solvent, N-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone is used to create a paste that is deposited on a 1 cm2 graphite sheet, which is then dried in a 60 °C oven for 20 h. In a potential window of 1.2 V, an electrode is evaluated using galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), cyclic voltammetry (CV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is also performed over a frequency range of 0.1–100 Hz.
The equations of specific capacitance (Cs), Energy and power densities of the electrode calculations are collected in S-1: Experimental Section (supporting information) [47,48].

X-ray diffractograms are performed to the two cellulosic origin porous carbon categories (hard and soft) cellulosic origin carbons. Activated carbon X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern is often impeded by its amorphous nature, making the detection of sharp peaks challenging. Consequently, broader peaks with a wider range are typically observed, which is an indicative of the micro-graphitic structure of the materials. In this study, Fig. 2 a and b represent the XRD diffractograms before and after activation process, respectively. Based on the JCPDS cards no: 6–6212 for graphite, 01-086-1561 for amorphous SiO2, and 47–1743 for calcite [49,50], it can be inferred that prior to activation, the samples consist of mineral crystals corresponding to these respective compounds beside graphitic structure as represented in Fig. 2a. Upon activation, the calcite (CaCO3) decomposes into calcium oxide (CaO) which turns to soluble Calcium ions (Ca) formed during washing and removed, while silicon ions (Si) persist in the activated samples as trace elements. XRD patterns of all the activated samples are shown in Fig. 2b, revealing two relatively broad peaks at around 23.7°–29° and 43°- 44.5° (2θ), which correspond to hexagonal graphite diffractions (002) and (100) (JCPDS card no. 41–1487) [21,51–53]. The high intensity of these peaks indicates a high degree of crystallinity, particularly prior to the activation process. Broader peaks at 5.9°–12° may be attributed to the (001) plane of Graphene Oxide, which may be formed due to the separation of a one layer of graphitic structure at high temperatures [21,51–53].

The characteristic peak intensity of the carbonized samples was significantly reduced and broadened, suggesting a decrease in the graphitization order of the samples at higher activation temperatures. This phenomenon may be attributed to the destruction of the atomic arrangement by the KOH activation process and a higher temperature. Furthermore, the reduction in diffraction peak intensity observed upon KOH activation can be ascribed to the existence of a turbostratic carbon skeleton characterized by randomly oriented graphene oxide layers in the activated samples. This signifies a notably enhanced level of porosity in comparison to the non-activated samples. Nevertheless, micro-graphitic structures were observed in all samples at Fig. 2b. The high temperature in some types of hard cellulosic origin carbonaceous materials such as Bn and Wn can cause the randomly appearance and the presence of KOH facilitate the turbostatic features and may release one of the graphene layers, making graphene oxide broader peak as shown in the WAC sample [21].

The average crystallite size, calculated using the Scherrer equation across the 3 mentioned planes (001), (002) and (100), was estimated to be lowest (WAC, 24.97 nm) and highest (GAC, 34.97 nm) as the calculated the crystallite sizes of the activated samples are summarized in Table S1, and S-2: Structural Characterization (XRD) (supporting information) [54].

The qualitative identification of activated cellulosic samples’ surface functional groups was achieved using an FTIR spectrometer spanning the range of 4000–400 cm−1. FTIR spectral analysis was employed to probe the structural characteristics and functional groups present. In Fig. 2c, the activated samples' FTIR spectra reveal prominent peaks, notably at approximately 3695 cm−1, corresponding to O–H stretching, and at 2916 cm−1, indicative of C–H stretching vibrations. Additionally, there are discernible shoulder peaks at 1958 cm−1 and 1608 cm−1, ascribed to stretching vibrations associated with CC and C–C bonds, respectively [55]. Three relatively less intense peaks at 1556 cm−1, 1475 cm−1, and 1139 cm−1 are linked to stretching vibrations of CO, C–OH, and C–O–C, in that order [55]. Furthermore, a peak related to coupled vibrations of Si+3, and Si+4 ions stretching is observed at 472 cm−1 [55]. The presence of these peaks signifies that the activated samples predominantly contain carboxyl groups, hydroxyl groups, and aryl ether groups integrated into the aromatic carbon structure. This result confirms the success of the activation process in converting cellulose and hemicellulose to activated carbons [56].

The Fluorescence (FL) measurements were conducted to elucidate the behavior of the obtained CQDs, fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescence intensity. The fluorescent characteristics of carbon quantum dots were evaluated at 290 nm excitation, revealing a prominent 319 nm emission maximum. Intensity serves as a precise method for quantum yield determination as represented at Fig. S1. The fluorescence mechanism of the prepared CQDs originates from two distinct sources: fluorescence arising from bandgap transitions within conjugated π domains and fluorescence stemming from surface defects. The peak at 319 nm indicates that the core of CQDs emit fluorescence in the visible spectrum's blue region. This emission peak indicates that CQDs have an energy bandgap and emit light at this wavelength when excited. The unique size, composition, and surface properties of the CQDs, as mentioned in the other characterization analysis, can be attributed to the specific energy level associated with this peak [40,57].

The presence of this peak indicates that CQDs have a broad emission spectrum and can emit light at various energy levels. An additional origin of fluorescence emission in CQDs results from surface defects, which emit fluorescence. Surface defects represent sites where the sp2 domains are imperfect and function as energy traps on the surface. Both the sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon atoms within the CQDs, along with functionalized surface defects, can contribute to fluorescence emission. The defects introduced in the CQDs' surface are due to oxidation that can serve as sites for capturing excitons. With an escalation in the degree of oxidation, an increased quantity of defects manifests on the surface of CQDs, leading to a reduction in energy level gaps. Fluorescence emission is a consequence of the recombination of electron-hole pairs within the sp2 domains, characterized by localized π-π∗ levels situated between the state bandgaps of the s-s∗ sp3 matrix. Surface passivation and functionalization contribute to the stabilization of surface defects, promoting more efficient electron recombination [40,57,58].

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) is a commonly utilized method for evaluating the thermal stability of carbon-based materials, and the TGA spectra of the prepared materials are illustrated in Fig. 2d.

The obtained spectra exhibit three distinct stages. The initial decrease is attributed to the removal of adsorbed water molecules, occurring at approximately 94 °C, followed by a low reduction in mass, which may be due to the degradation of hemicellulose and cellulose in the prepared samples which occurred at temperatures between 100 °C and 600 °C, and the third stage, which happens at higher temperatures is associated with lignin degradation [59].

All carbonaceous materials exhibited a similar behavior in their TGA spectra, comprising three distinct stages of weight loss. The samples demonstrated the highest weight loss between 59 % and 82 % at temperatures ranging from 75 °C to 630 °C, with residual masses ranging from 53 % to 75 %. These findings are collected and summarized in Table S2.

Raman spectra of the prepared samples are depicted in Fig. 2e revealing three distinct bands situated at approximately 1340 cm−1, 1580 cm−1, and 2900 cm−1. These bands can primarily be assigned to the D-band (indicating disorder and defects), the G-band (associated with graphitic structures), and the 2D-band (indicative of order), respectively. The D-band typically signifies disordered vibrational modes within the sp2 lattice structure of carbon materials, highlighting defects or structural imperfections in the samples. The G-band, on the other hand, results from the hybrid carbon atoms' in-plane vibrations, while the 2D-band is associated with two-phonon resonance, indicating an overall trend toward ordered structures in the prepared samples [60]. In the case of OAC, JAC, and BAC, the Raman spectra exhibit a shift of the 2D band to 2600 cm−1, accompanied by the presence of a 2D′ band at 2900 cm−1. The 2D′ band may represent an overtone of the D band or be associated with other combination modes involving phonon vibrations. Additionally, this band may be indicative of different phases of carbon present in the samples like carbon nanohair, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [60]. Further, the D-band to G-band intensities ratio (ID/IG) is often used to characterize carbon materials' degree of amorphousness or defect degree. The higher relative intensity (ID/IG) values of BAC, PAC, WAC, GAC, JAC, OAC are (1, 0.93, 0.89, 0.92, 0.97, 0.92), respectively. As can be seen, the higher (ID/IG) calculations range from 1 (BAC, highest) to 0.89 (WAC, lowest) indicate a low graphitic order plane of these treated carbons providing a high amorphous and confirming a high developed porosity [60].

The crystallite size (La) of the prepared composites, measured in nanometers, is determined using the following equation [54]:

Where, λ is s the laser excitation wavelength (514.5 nm).

The calculated crystallite sizes of BAC, PAC, WAC, GAC, JAC, and OAC are 16.81 nm, 18.08 nm, 18.89 nm, 18.28 nm, 17.34 nm, and 18.28 nm, respectively. Using the aforementioned formula, we determined that the crystallite size ranges from a maximum of 16.81 nm in WAC to a minimum of 18.89 nm in BAC.

Based on the XRD analysis in Fig. 2b, we hypothesized the presence of graphene oxide in several samples, potentially segregating from the graphitic structure. To more precisely assess the presence of graphene in this carbonaceous composite, we calculated the I2D/IG ratio, which serves as an indicator of the number of graphene layers in the samples [54]. The BAC sample demonstrated a maximum I2D/IG ratio of 0.96, indicating a higher presence of defects and fewer graphene layers. In contrast, the JAC sample recorded a minimum I2D/IG ratio of 0.55, suggesting fewer defects and a greater number of graphene layers compared to other samples. The arrangement of samples from higher to lower I2D/IG ratio is: BAC = 0.96, PAC = 0.69, OAC = 0.67, GAC = 0.65, WAC = 0.57, and JAC = 0.55, respectively.

The analysis of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to ascertain the chemical states and elemental composition of the prepared samples, confirming their characteristics. Fig. 3 presents the XPS survey scan of the GAC and JAC samples, revealing the presence of four principal peaks at specific binding energies: GAC (285.52, 533.49, 400.4, and 104.17) eV and JAC (285.52, 533.43, 399.35, and 104.11) eV, corresponding to C1s, O1s, N1s, and Si2p, respectively. Table 1 provides details on the elemental detection, valence states, and ratios of these elements. The C1s spectrum exhibits distinct peaks representing sp2 CC (GAC 284.41, JAC 248.48) eV, sp3 C–C, and C–H (GAC 285.34, JAC 286.18) eV, and CO (GAC 289.48, 288.85) eV. The O1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into C–OH and C–O–C (GAC 532.64, 532.72) eV, O–CO (GAC 530.92, JAC 531.25) eV [61]. Moreover, most of the oxygen atoms are primarily existing in binding states associated with groups of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl, suggesting a potential contribution to increased capacitance in the form of pseudo-capacitance. Chemisorbed oxygen (H2O & O2) is observed at GAC 533.63 eV and JAC 534.09 eV, indicating a relatively higher chemical adsorption of oxygen in JAC compared to GAC. However, the GAC sample exhibits a higher C/O ratio, approximately twice that of JAC, which improves active materials' electrochemical performance [62]. Nitrogen (N) is present in trace amounts as CN or C–N species. Additionally, traces of silicate, originating from the waste material itself, can be detected, appearing as two species: Si+3 and Si+4 [63]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology of the prepared samples by scanning electron microscopy (SEM)
ce:section.ce:para: SEM images represented in Fig. 4 show the morphology of the prepared composites from different cellulosic origins before and after activation process at 15.000× SEM magnification.

The prepared samples' morphology shows that the process of activation increases porosity of the materials and make the surface more roughness as one can find in Fig. 4 the conversion between a to a∗. As the garlic leaves morphology after high temperature treatment of 500 °C give a structure of porous graphitic carbon with high porosity, which increased by the chemical treatment at 800 °C. The same behavior is seen in other studied samples. The morphology of pores looks broken and irregular, and the surface of samples turns from smooth to rough with thermal-chemical activation [28].

Most of pore's structures are from the nature of the carbonaceous material origin itself and by high temperature of pyrolysis followed by the chemical activation by potassium hydroxide in high temperature give it a microporosity and a roughness structure which increase the surface area. The discovery of microporosity, CQDs has been identified as a crucial factor in enhancing both diffusivity and the overall performance of the supercapacitor [14].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology of the prepared samples by transmission electron microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Fig. 5 a–b, the images obtained by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of the JAC and OAC samples reveal the presence of transparent, large, and relatively thick layers of activated carbon and Few layers of graphene. These layers are adorned with an abundance of carbon nanohairs and a few carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and the size of CQDs is found to range from 2 to 7 nm. A more detailed view in the high-resolution TEM image (HR-TEM) is shown in Fig. 5b–c for JAC, and Fig. 5f for OAC confirm the existence of sp3 hybridization, a result of structural distortions occurring during the thermal synthesis and chemical activation processes.

Furthermore, the electron diffraction pattern in the chosen area displayed in Fig. 5g exhibits a clear amorphous pattern with its corresponding planes, affirming the presence of disordered vibrational modes within the sp2 lattice structure of carbon materials. It is discerned that the activation method impacts the structure and porosity. Morphological analysis suggests that the prepared materials offer a substantially increased surface area, a finding supported by nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm measurements.

EDX analysis shows a high carbon content in the range of 91.96 %, a low oxygen and silicon of 5.78 % and 2.53 %, respectively, besides traces of sodium as shown in the mapping STEM images in the right-hand side of Fig. 5. Carbon nanohair is detected with an average diameter of about 5 nm and d-spacing of 0.345 nm as shown in Fig. 5c, d, and 5e. Selected area electron diffraction of the JAC sample is shown in Fig. 5g with the rings of (001), (002) and (100) planes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The pore structures and specific surface area
ce:section.ce:para: The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm profiles, BET plots, and BJH plots for the prepared AC composites collectively demonstrate the characteristics of type-I isotherms, in accordance with the IUPAC classification, as depicted in Fig. 6 . Particularly noteworthy is the pronounced elevation at low relative pressure on the adsorption curve (P/P0 < 0.1), signifying the substantial existence of numerous micropores [48]. Both micropores and mesopores play a substantial role in determining activated carbon's adsorption capacity. Micropores are essential for adsorption, while mesopores are particularly effective for accommodating larger molecules during the adsorption process.

The surface areas and textural characteristics of the activated carbons (ACs) were determined from the various sources using a consistent activator and activation temperatures. In line with IUPAC guidelines, pores within porous materials are classified by size into micropores (less than 2 nm), mesopores (2–50 nm), and macropores (greater than 50 nm) [64]. The analysis of the prepared porous carbon samples primarily revealed a microporous structure, as evident from pore diameters on average presented in Table S3, which shows that the total pore volume and BET surface area of the activated carbon composites have increased. The high treatment temperature plays a crucial role in enhancing the surface area by promoting the formation of micropores as well as facilitating the activation process and partial structure ordering. The BET surface areas varied between 1160 m2 g−1 and 1620 m2 g−1, depending on their initial morphology. The micropore volumes of the ACs ranged from 0.5595 cm3 g−1 to 0.8203 cm3 g−1. The BJH plots at Fig. 6 elucidated microporosity of the prepared samples within the specified range of average pore diameters from 1.7823 to 2.0203 nm.

The results derived from N2 adsorption/desorption experiments for all ACs indicate that these porous samples possess a consistent capacity for the adsorption and release of N2 gas, as evidenced by nearly identical adsorption and desorption isotherms. This attribute renders them well-suited for a range of uses, including energy storage, hydrogen storage, and adsorption processes, supported by their pore structure analysis. Micropores aid double layer formation in energy storage, although they may somewhat impede the kinetics of ion transport. Mesopores, on the other hand, help reduce the resistance to ion diffusion, while macropores assist in establishing ion reservoirs, thereby decreasing the distance over which ions need to traverse after electrolyte penetration.

The effect of Zeta potential was examined for the prepared composites. The outcomes revealed that the Zeta potential of the prepared materials was negative surface charge and had a high magnitude, indicating strong electrostatic attraction between positively charged medium and the carbon surface [65]. This finding was consistent with the observed increase in efficiency with decreasing pH and in acidic electrolytes, which can increase the availability of H+ ions to electrostatically attract negatively charged carbon materials [65]. The zeta sizer analysis revealed particle sizes on average distribution ranging from 218 nm (lowest, for WAC) to 942 nm (highest, for BAC), which are in line with the aggregates observed in the SEM micrographs in Fig. 4. The zeta sizer findings of the prepared samples were plotted in Fig. 2f. The prepared composites reveals a poor electronic conductivity which ranging from 0.0662 ms/cm of GAC (highest) to 0.00412 ms/cm of OAC (lowest), all characteristic data of zeta potential, electronic conductivity and zeta sizer are collected in Table S4.

The generation of activated carbon from agricultural and industrial wastes is commonly justified by two factors. Firstly, the distinctive characteristics of these precursors and secondly, the mass production potential through physical and chemical activation. Additionally, activated carbons derived from high-temperature char have a greater total volume, surface area, and microporous volume compared to those derived from low-temperature char. The primary goal of activation is to produce and increase the porosity of the carbon material as represented in Fig. 7 [26,30], thereby enhancing its adsorptive capacity. Typically, the precursor is carbonized as equation (2), and the reaction of equation (3) occurs at temperatures between 400 and 800 °C. When the temperature of the reaction is 500 °C, the KOH has been completely consumed [66].

Subsequently, K2CO3, as generated in accordance with equation (3), initiates a decomposition process described by equation (3) at temperatures exceeding 700 °C. This decomposition reaction reaches its conclusion at 800 °C, concomitant with the occurrence of reactions represented by equation (3) to equation (7). The potassium vapor, produced in accordance with equation 6, and 7, infiltrates the interstices within the specially crafted carbon lattice. During the carbonization or pyrolysis of agricultural or textile waste, small molecular fragments rich in carbon and oxygen are formed. Under high temperature and pressure, these fragments undergo nucleation and surface passivation, leading to the formation of CQDs [67]. The thermal decomposition process facilitates the formation of CQDs among other carbonaceous products. Concurrently, the high temperatures and pressures serve as catalysts for the deposition of carbon atoms, thereby initiating the nucleation and subsequent growth of these atoms into carbon nanohair structures. Carbon nanohairs are typically formed due to the localized catalytic and stress-induced growth on carbon surfaces. When certain regions of the precursor have non-uniform decomposition, fiber-rich structures may lead to whisker-like carbon nanohairs. These nanohairs emerge during the thermal shock and activation steps, especially at defect sites [68]. Following the elimination of potassium salts through a washing procedure as in equation (8), a novel structural arrangement emerges, giving rise to the formation of numerous pores, thereby yielding a state of ultra-microporosity [30,69]. For this study, we utilized potassium hydroxide as a chemical activator, which can eliminate oxygen from the alkali, cross-linking removal and stabilizing carbon atoms in the crystallites, whereas K metal can be intercalated and washed easily leaving the microporous structure. CQDs are embedded within the pores or on the surface of the carbon and CNHs decorate the surface of the activated carbon backbone, increasing surface area and roughness. They create hierarchical porosity and facilitate ion-accessibility. Our composite production employs an entirely eco-friendly activation process, yielding products with a negative carbon impact. The resulting byproduct, syngas (CO + H2)g, not only serves as an environmental catalyst, but also fosters hydrogen production a testament to our commitment to sustainable and efficient processes. Subsequently, the aforementioned hypothesis can be validated in detail by morphology characterization and BET analyses.

Carbonization at 500 °C:
(2) Cellulosic materials (s) → C (s) (Biochar)

Activation at 800 °C:
(3) 2C + 6KOH → 2K + 2K2CO3 + 3H2
(4) K2CO3 → K2O + CO2
(5) CO2 + C → 2CO
(6) K2CO3 + 2C → 2K + 3CO
(7) K2O + C → 2K + CO

Washing:
(8) C + 2K2CO3 + 3 HCl → KOH + 3KCl + H2O + 2CO2 ↑ + C (s) ↓

ce:section.ce:section-title: The assessment of properties of electrochemistry
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical properties of various carbon materials, including WAC, PAC, BAC, GAC, OAC, and JAC, were evaluated employing cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) measurements, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Results are summarized and collected in Fig. 8 and Tables S6, S7, and S8. Fig. 8 depicts the effect of carbonaceous origin on the CV, GCD, and EIS curves. The results indicate that JAC, and WAC revealed the highest specific capacitance in addition to the best current response in comparison to others. This implies excellent capacitive behavior, associated with the favorable surface structure, surface area, and pore occupancy, at 0.5 A g−1 current density and a potential window extending to 1.2 V in the case of WAC. Notably, carbon materials possessing ultra-microporous structure cause de-solvation of electrolyte ions upon adsorption within these confined spaces, leading to an exceptionally high specific capacitance. JAC possesses its highest capacity at 0.9 V potential window. Moreover, OAC has a good capacitive behavior in a potential window of 1.2 V. This is contributed by JAC that has the lowest pore size with high surface area compared to others [28].

The longer charge-discharge time of WAC compared to the other samples demonstrates its higher capacitive properties as shown in Fig. 8e. This observation is corroborated by the GCD measurements, which support the CV findings as represented in Fig. 8e∗. The cyclic voltammetry curves (CV) for the WAC electrode at different scan rates display an almost symmetrical rectangular shape, suggesting the prevalent behavior of an electric double-layer capacitor (EDLC) and a rapid electrochemical response. Intriguingly, this quasi-rectangular shape persists even at elevated scan rates, such as 200 mV s−1, highlighting the rapid ion diffusion characteristics facilitated by the perfect porous structure and the minimal polarization tolerance of the WAC electrode [70]. Moreover, the galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) plots manifest quasi-isosceles triangular patterns, affirming the prevalent presence of an electric double-layer capacitor (EDLC) type with minimal internal resistance (IR). The nearly symmetric triangular configurations imply extra pseudocapacitor behavior attributed to the reversible redox reactions linked to the oxygen content within the carbonaceous material framework, as previously mentioned in the FTIR characterization and represented in Fig. 2c. Significantly, the GCD plots exhibit symmetrical shapes for all samples, devoid of any noticeable IR drop. This suggests excellent carrier transport throughout the material and an almost ideal EDLC-like quasi-triangular pattern during the charge-discharge process [70]. Furthermore, in accordance with Equations (1)–(4) in supporting information, the electrode-specific capacitance (Cs) values for WAC, OAC, BAC, PAC, and GAC were determined as 245.83, 187.5, 119.17, 55, and 40.83 F g−1, respectively whereas the specific capacitance values for cell (Ccell) were determined as 983.3, 750, 476.6, 220, and 163 F g−1, respectively, when operating within a potential window of 1.2 V and a 0.5 A g−1 current density. In contrast, the electrode-specific capacitance, and the cell-specific capacitance of JAC at a 0.5 A g−1 current density and a 0.9 V potential window, was measured at 247.22, and 988.8 F g−1, respectively. Specific capacitance, energy density, and power density values have been compiled and are presented in Tables S6 and S7, and the relation between current densities and the obtained specific capacitance presented in Fig. S2. Notably, the obtained specific capacitance values for these various electrode materials surpass those reported for recent carbon materials derived from biomass. [28,48,56,71]. Reasons behind different performance from different raw materials: The variation in performance stems from the inherent differences in the structural and chemical properties of the raw materials used. Each raw material has distinct porosity, surface area, and functional groups, which influence its activation behavior and interaction with the electrolytes during the electrochemical process. Furthermore, the elemental composition and the thermal stability of the materials affect their conductivity and overall electrochemical performance. By choosing different raw materials, the final carbon nanohair/CQDs/activated carbon composite exhibits unique characteristics that contribute to variations in performance.

When investigating the relationship between the carbon samples' specific capacitance values (Cs) at varying constant currents, ranging from 0.5 A g−1 to 10 A g−1, it is observed that the Cs values of the JAC sample reduced from 247.22 to 188.89, and for the cell reduced from 988.8 to 755.6 F g−1. This indicates a higher-rate capability of 76.4 % for the JAC sample, as compared to the rate capabilities of the WAC (71.3 %), OAC (44.4 %), GAC (20.4 %), BAC (35 %), and PAC (16.5 %) samples. The value of the specific capacitance obtained of GAC electrode was potential window dependent as GAC in very low potential window (0.4 V and 0.5 V) has a high discharge time and better than others reported at 0.8 V and 1.2 V, the specific capacitance values of GAC in (0.4, 0.5, 0.8, and 1.2) V potential window were (337.5, 1574, 185, 40.83) F.g−1 respectively, and the capacitance values for GAC cells' were determined (1350, 6296, 740, and 163.3) F.g−1 respectively, with a capability ratio 3 %, 45 %, 20.3 % at potential window (0.4, 0.8, 1.2) V respectively at 20 A g−1, which appears that the specific capacitance initially increases from 0.4 V to 0.5 V. However, beyond 0.5 V, the specific capacity begins to decrease as the potential window increases further. This phenomenon can be occurred due to the occurrence of a favorable electrochemical reaction at the electrode-electrolyte interface within the low potential window range, so the specific capacity increases because of more charges can be stored in the electrode material. However, beyond a potential window of 0.5 V, the Cs starts to decrease, and this can be attributed to several reasons; at higher potentials; some of undesirable side reaction may occur at the electrode-electrolyte interface and this leads to reduce the available charge storage capacity. Also, higher potentials can cause electrode degradation and this leads to structure and chemical reaction changes and results in decreasing the specific capacitance of the sample [72]. All these reasons can add to the material itself and it may has limitations in the terms of ion insertion/extraction kinetics at higher potentials, all the data are collected in Table S8 and the relation between the potential window and obtained specific capacitance of GAC is plotted in Fig. S3. The porous structure and high yield CQDs presence are also responsible for the highest value of GAC's specific capacitance at low potentials [73]. As GAC is composed of a large number of small pores, which creates a high surface area. The pores are filled with ions and electrolytes, which can move freely within the pores when an electric field is applied. At low potentials, the ions and electrolytes move easily through the pores, resulting in a high capacitance value. However, as the potential increases, the ions and electrolytes become more difficult to move within the pores, resulting in a decrease in specific capacitance value. This behavior is known as "ion-blocking" or "electrostatic double-layer capacitance." [74]. In addition, the high surface area of GAC also contributes to its high capacitance value at low potentials. The large number of pores and high surface area allow for efficient electrostatic double-layer formation, which results in a high specific capacitance value. Particularly its high specific capacitance at low potential windows, indicates efficient charge storage with minimal resistive losses. This behavior directly enhances the power density in short voltage ranges, as power density benefits from rapid ion transport. As a result of the high surface area, the ultra-microporous structure distribution, and the carbon skeleton itself, the high capacitances are advantageous for energy storage applications, allowing for efficient energy storage and release. GAC can store a large amount of energy due to its high specific capacitance value at low potentials, while its low specific capacitance value at high potentials allows for efficient energy release [33].

Spectroscopy of electrochemical impedance (EIS) was also conducted on the prepared samples to probe the electrolyte ion diffusion process and transport kinetics. The Nyquist plots of the working electrode materials, as illustrated in Fig. 8c, exhibit two distinct regions: The first region consisted of a minute semi-circle in the range of high frequencies, associated with the electron transfer mechanism. This segment pertained to electrode conductivity and charge transfer resistance, with the diameter of the semi-circle corresponding to the resistance to interfacial charge transfer, denoted as Rct. The second region comprised of a linear segment within the low-frequency spectrum, which was linked to the resistance of ion diffusion in the electrolytes within the electrode. This part represented the Warburg resistance (ZW), indicating the diffusion/transport of ions into active materials. These characteristics are typical for electrode materials based on porous activated carbon.

Analysis of the EIS curves revealed that the very small semi-circle appeared in the EIS plot's high-frequency region exclusively in the BAC electrode (Rct = 4.8 Ω), accompanied by an internal resistance (Rs = 0.9 Ω). This resistance component comprised of the contact electrode materials' resistance and the resistance of ion transport within the electrolyte. Additionally, a vertical line close to 90 °C in the low-frequency part indicated rapid ion transport, linked to the high electronic conductivity and enhanced ion diffusion properties of BAC [71]. It's worth mentioning that the rest of the prepared carbon electrodes did not exhibit any observable semicircular features. This absence of semicircles can be attributed to the fact that the majority of porous carbon materials primarily store charges through physical means without undergoing significant charge transfer. They essentially act as genuine supercapacitors (EDLC), signifying that all of the reactive sites are promptly accessible, resulting in a capacitor-like behavior with high capacitance values [28,70].

Carbon electrode materials' electrochemical properties were additionally assessed through the measurement of cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves, using a three-electrode configuration. The CV curves obtained at various scan rates consistently exhibited a shape that is quasi-rectangular, confirming the dominant behavior of electric double-layer capacitors (EDLC) [70]. The ideal operating potential window for most of the prepared materials was found to fall within the range of 0–1.2 V. As a result, the charge storage mechanism attributed to the electrode materials is unequivocally linked to EDLC behavior, characterized by a quasi-rectangular shape, as depicted in Fig. 8e∗, 8f∗, 8g∗, 8h∗, 8i∗, and 8j∗. Both agricultural and textile wastes have been explored as potential materials for supercapacitors, but their suitability depends on factors such as carbon content, surface area, and porosity. Preliminary results suggest that agricultural waste, due to its high lignocellulosic content, tends to produce more porous carbon nanohair/CQDs/activated carbon composite with a higher surface area, making it more suitable for supercapacitor applications. However, specific textile waste types with high cellulose or natural fiber content can also perform well after proper treatment and activation.

The textile waste may contain various elements that could impact performance. However, we apply both physical and chemical treatments to the textile waste, which results in a pure composite of carbon nanohair/CQDs/activated carbon. The final composite contains trace amounts of silica, nitrogen, and oxygen, which do not significantly affect the cyclic voltammetry performance, as they either enhance conductivity or contribute to surface functionality without interfering with the electrochemical behavior.

This let us know, how specific structural characteristics, such as surface area, pore size distribution, and functional groups, correlate directly with the material's electrochemical properties, including capacitance, rate capability, and cycling stability. This will provide a more balanced and comprehensive view of both the material's physical and electrochemical properties.

The cyclic electrochemical stability of the JAC supercapacitor electrode was evaluated using the constant current charge-discharge method in 0.5 M H2SO4. Fig. S4 displays the capacitance retention of the supercapacitor electrode over multiple cycles. The JAC electrode demonstrates outstanding cyclic stability, with capacitance retention increasing up to ∼260 % over 1200 cycles. This increase in real capacitance could be due to several factors: Firstly, over time and with cycling, the surface of the JAC undergoes electrochemical activation, increasing the effective surface area available for charge storage. This activation can lead to a rise in capacitance beyond the initial value. Secondly, the presence of sulfuric acid facilitates pseudocapacitive behavior of the embedded heteroatoms in the JAC structure as soon as carbon nanohair and CQDs interact with the electrolyte to provide additional pseudocapacitive effects [80,81]. Additionally, the presence of functional groups and the porous structure of the electrode enable better utilization of the active material over time, resulting in increased capacitance [82]. Furthermore, carbon nanohair and CQDs maintain their structural integrity over long-term cycling, preventing degradation and ensuring consistent performance, and the incorporation of carbon dots enhances electrochemical performance by increasing charge storage capacity, improving conductivity, promoting ion transport, and stabilizing the electrode structure for longer cycle life [83,84]. After 1200 cycles, the capacitance retention% value is decreased gradually to reach ∼220 % after 2000 cycles, this decrease may due to the available active sites are occupied with charge and started to form a passive layer which can thicken over time, increase charge transfer resistance and change the diffusion impedance. These results are clearly detected by the difference in electrode impedance before and after the cyclic stability test, as shown in Fig. S4 (Supporting information).

Regarding the structure formed during the electrode preparation, we did not observe significant stacking issues during electrode formation. The material's porosity and surface area help prevent excessive stacking, maintaining efficient ion transport and charge storage. The cyclic stability observed is consistent with findings from studies such as those referenced [85,86]. Our study discussed similar behaviors observed in layered structures, where enhanced structural integrity and mechanical stability contribute to improved cycling performance and the results align with these observations, showing that the structural characteristics of our composite promote robust cycling stability. The referenced findings support the conclusion that the design of the electrode material minimizes stacking issues while promoting excellent electrochemical performance and cyclic stability [85,86].

According to Table 1. The comparison of smart materials derived from various biomass sources, focusing on their specific capacitance and cyclic stability, the carbon nanohair composite in the current study offers competitive capacitance (988.8 F g-1) with an unusual capacitance increase up to ∼260 % after 1200 cycles. These findings underscore the potential of biomass-derived materials for supercapacitor applications.

At the end of this study, we offer a professional and sustainable approach to repurpose agricultural waste and old clothes, transforming them into valuable activated carbon materials suitable for use in supercapacitors, for example, energy storage devices as eco-friendly electrode with no need to add more toxic electrode materials or expensive other to get high specific capacitance values. Our findings shed light on the feasibility of an energy-efficient and environmentally friendly method for producing high-performance electrode materials for practical applications as represented in Fig. 8b, all the carbonaceous materials possess a superpower density and an optimum energy density with a high specific capacitance that can reach to around thousand or above according to the potential window applied, this leads a high range of energy storage applications.

In this study, we have successfully synthesized a composite of carbon nanohair/CQDs/activated carbon. Notably, this is the first instance where such a composite has been developed as well as the first time of activated carbon to be synthesized through this cost-effective and eco-friendly method that obviates the need for inert gas. As a result, enhancing porosity, surface area, and morphology. Measurements of the obtained specific surface area ranged from 1620 m2/g (OAC, highest) to 1160 m2/g (GAC, lowest), JAC demonstrates an outstanding electrode-specific capacitance of 247.22 at 0.5 A/g and 0.9 V, with a capacitance retention increasing up to ∼ 260 % over 1200 cycles. This is related to its unique structure. In this study, GAC has a reverse relationship between specific capacitance and higher potential window, with a notably high electrode-specific capacitance (Cs) value of 1574 F/g observed at a low potential window of 0.5 V. As the potential window increases to a moderate level of 0.8 V, the capacitance value decreases to 185 F/g. At a higher potential window of 1.2 V, the capacitance value is substantially reduced to 40.83 F/g. This behavior highlights GAC's ability to deliver high energy and power densities concurrently. The study highlights the potential of the ACs produced through this cost-effective synthesis method emerging as highly promising electrode materials for supercapacitors, thanks to their compelling electrochemical performance and hierarchical porous structure decorated with carbon nanohair and CQDs. The carbon nanohair's remarkable effect: notably, even at a significantly higher current density of 10 A/g, the material exhibits an impressive JAC's capacitance retention of 76.4 %, affirming its suitability for high-performance energy storage applications. The synergistic effects of the carbon nanohair structure and CQDs contribute to the enhanced electrochemical performance of the activated carbon, making it a promising candidate for advanced energy storage technologies. This research aligns with circular economy and SDGs by sustainably producing activated carbon from agricultural waste and addresses SDGs 2 (Zero Hunger) and 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy) by utilizing activated carbon composites for energy storage, supporting renewable energy. It also aligns with SDG 12 (Responsible Consumption and Production) by promoting waste management and resource efficiency. Additionally, it contributes to SDGs 13 (Climate Action) and 15 (Life on Land) through carbon sequestration and waste conversion. So, this work paves the way for further advances in the development of high-performance supercapacitors using a cost effective environmentally friendly methods and ecologically sustainable materials, contributing to the growing demand for sustainable energy storage solutions.

In this study, it is strongly recommended that carbon nanohair/CQDs/activated carbon composite represents a multifaceted material with a myriad of promising applications. Within the energy storage domain, AC's composites exhibit heightened capacitance and rapid charge-discharge characteristics, rendering it an exceptional candidate for supercapacitors, energy storage systems, and electric vehicles. Extending its utility beyond the realm of energy, and proves highly effective in environmental remediation applications, demonstrating significant adsorption capabilities for pollutants in water and air. Furthermore, AC's applicability extends to catalysis, serving as an exceptional support material for various catalytic processes. In addition, AC particularly when adorned with CQDs and carbon nanohair, reveals substantial potential in the field of sensors and biosensors, thereby contributing to advancements in analytical chemistry and medical diagnostics. Its adaptability also extends to gas separation and purification applications, harnessing the material's versatile properties for use across a wide spectrum of industrial and research contexts.
